prs.title: fix: correct Google Fonts URLs on landing page
prs.body: ## Description This PR corrects the Google Fonts stylesheet URLs used on the landing page. The updated URLs ensure the required font stylesheets are loaded successfully, allowing the intended typography to be applied consistently across the landing page. ## Type of Change - [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change that fixes an issue) - [ ] New feature (non-breaking change that adds functionality) - [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to change) - [ ] Documentation update ## Related Issue Fixes #2076 ## Checklist - [x] My code follows the project's style guidelines - [x] I have performed a self-review of my code - [x] No additional code comments were required for this change - [x] No documentation updates were required for this change - [x] My changes generate no new warnings - [ ] I have added tests that prove my fix/feature works - [x] New and existing tests pass locally ## Screenshots (if applicable) ### Before - Font stylesheet requests for the landing page did not load successfully. - The intended fonts were not applied consistently. ### After - Font stylesheet requests load successfully. - The landing page renders with the intended typography. ## Verification - Verified the landing page loads successfully. - Verified the Google Fonts stylesheet requests return **HTTP 200** in the browser Network tab. - Verified the Cinzel and Inter fonts are applied correctly after the update. ## Additional Notes This change is limited to updating the Google Fonts stylesheet URLs and does not affect any other application functionality.

prs.title: fix: remove duplicate theme.js import causing theme toggle issues
prs.body: ## Description This PR fixes a landing page issue where the theme toggle was not functioning correctly due to duplicate imports of `theme.js`. During investigation of Issue #2052, it was found that `game/templates/game/landing.html` imported `theme.js` twice: * `{% static 'game/js/theme.js' %}` * `{% static 'game/js/theme.js' %}?v=1` This caused duplicate initialization of the theme toggle functionality and multiple click listeners to be attached to `#themeToggle`, resulting in inconsistent theme switching behavior. The duplicate import has been removed, leaving a single versioned import and ensuring the theme toggle is initialized only once. ## Type of Change * [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change that fixes an issue) * [ ] New feature (non-breaking change that adds functionality) * [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to change) * [ ] Documentation update ## Related Issue Fixes #2052 ## Checklist * [x] My code follows the project's style guidelines * [x] I have performed a self-review of my code * [x] No additional code comments were required for this change * [x] No documentation updates were required for this change * [x] My changes generate no new warnings * [ ] I have added tests that prove my fix/feature works * [x] New and existing tests pass locally ## Screenshots (if applicable) ### Before * The landing page loaded `theme.js` twice. * Multiple click listeners were attached to `#themeToggle`. * Theme switching behavior was inconsistent due to duplicate initialization. ### After * Only one `theme.js` import remains on the landing page. * Only one click listener is attached to `#themeToggle`. * Theme toggle correctly switches between light and dark modes. * Theme preference persists correctly through localStorage. ### Verification Screenshots ## Verification * Verified only one `theme.js` import remains in `game/templates/game/landing.html`. * Verified the theme toggle correctly switches between light and dark modes. * Verified theme preference persists after page refresh. * Verified duplicate initialization no longer occurs. ## Additional Notes The root cause was a duplicate import of `theme.js` in `game/templates/game/landing.html`. Removing the redundant import resolved the issue and restored expected theme toggle behavior without affecting other functionality.

The duplicate import has been removed, leaving a single versioned import. ## Type of Change * [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change that fixes an issue) * [ ] New feature (non-breaking change that adds functionality) * [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to change) * [ ] Documentation update ## Related Issue Fixes #2052 ## Checklist * [x] My code follows the project's style guidelines * [x] I have performed a self-review of my code * [ ] I have commented my code where necessary (not applicable for this change) * [ ] I have updated the documentation if needed (not applicable for this change) * [x] My changes generate no new warnings * [ ] I have added tests that prove my fix/feature works * [x] New and existing tests pass locally ## Screenshots (if applicable) ### Before * The theme toggle was not functioning correctly on the landing page. * Duplicate initialization resulted in multiple click listeners being attached to `#themeToggle`. ### After * Only one instance of `theme.js` is loaded. * The theme toggle correctly switches between light and dark modes. ## Testing * Verified only one `theme.js` import remains on the landing page. * Verified only one click listener is attached to `#themeToggle`. * Verified the theme toggle correctly switches between light and dark modes. * Verified theme changes persist correctly after toggling. ## Additional Notes The root cause was a duplicate import of `theme.js` in `game/templates/game/landing.html`. prs.title: fix: improve recommendation input validation for unsupported values (#676)
prs.body:
## Summary

This PR improves recommendation input validation by adding stricter checks for unsupported values in recommendation inputs.

Previously, validation only checked for missing fields, which allowed unsupported values (such as invalid time availability, unsupported experience levels, or unsupported interests) to reach recommendation logic and potentially cause inconsistent behavior. This update validates allowed values and returns meaningful validation errors. Additional tests were added to cover invalid input cases.

## Related Issue

Closes #676

## Type of Change

- [x] Bug fix — resolves a broken behaviour
- [ ] Feature — adds new functionality
- [ ] Data — adds new projects to `data/projects.json`
- [ ] Documentation — updates docs, README, or code comments only
- [ ] Style — CSS or visual changes only, no logic change
- [ ] Refactor — restructures code without changing behaviour
- [x] Test — adds or updates tests

## What Was Changed

| File | Change made |
|------|-------------|
| `utils/recommender.py` | Added stricter validation for unsupported values in level, interest, and time availability |
| `tests/test_basic.py` | Added test cases for invalid recommendation inputs |

## How to Test This PR

1. Checkout this branch:
   ```bash
   git checkout fix/input-validation-676

2. Run tests:
```bash
python tests/test_basic.py
```

3. Verify:
- Invalid level values are rejected
- Invalid interest values are rejected
- Invalid time availability values are rejected
- Existing valid inputs continue working

Expected result:
```text
44 passed, 0 failed out of 44 tests
```

## Test Results

```text
44 passed, 0 failed out of 44 tests
```

## Self-Review Checklist

- [x] I have read CONTRIBUTING.md and followed guidelines
- [x] I ran tests locally
- [x] I have not introduced debug statements
- [x] I have not modified unrelated files outside issue scope

## Notes for Reviewer

This PR improves recommendation input validation by rejecting unsupported values and adds test coverage for invalid input cases.

prs.title: ci: add PR size warning workflow
prs.body:
Summary

This PR introduces a GitHub Action that warns when pull requests exceed 500 lines of changes.

Large PRs are harder to review and maintain. This workflow helps encourage contributors to submit smaller, more manageable pull requests.

Behavior
- If PR changes exceed 500 lines, a warning is shown in CI.
- This does not fail the CI run; it only provides a warning.

Benefits

- improves maintainability
- simplifies review process

prs.title: docs: Add comprehensive analysis of pointer events issue #56614

This PR adds 13 comprehensive documentation files (~40k words) that explain the resolution to GitHub issue #56614: 'Expectations in pointerevent_after_target_removed_from_slot.html'

## Documentation Includes:

**Quick Start Guides:**
- 00_START_HERE.md: Master summary and navigation
- QUICK_COMMANDS.md: Copy-paste terminal commands for PR submission
- QUICK_REFERENCE.md: Q&A, troubleshooting, and quick lookup

**Comprehensive Learning:**
- POINTER_EVENTS_FIX_SUMMARY.md: Technical overview of the fix
- DETAILED_ANALYSIS.md: In-depth analysis of the problem
- COMPLETE_SOLUTION.md: Full explanation of all aspects
- SOLUTION_COMPLETE.md: Complete solution overview

**Contributing & Guidance:**
- HOW_TO_CREATE_PR.md: Step-by-step PR creation guide
- PULL_REQUEST_GUIDE.md: Contributing patterns to WPT
- GITHUB_ISSUE_GUIDE.md: How to file and reference issues
- PR_SUBMISSION_GUIDE.md: Complete PR submission workflow
- README_DOCUMENTATION.md: Documentation index and navigation
- INDEX_AND_SUMMARY.md: Quick reference index

## What This Explains:

✅ The original issue with pointer events in shadow DOM ✅ Why tests were failing in all browsers
✅ How the fix aligns with W3C specifications
✅ Three test scenarios: remove-slot, remove-filler, change-slotname ✅ Event retargeting and boundary event behavior
✅ Privacy implications of relatedTarget
✅ How to understand and extend these concepts
✅ Step-by-step guides for contributors

## Related Issues:
- Fixes #56614
- References commit a9f4351e61 (the actual fix)
- PR #55894 (original PR that fixed the issue)

## For Reviewers:

These docs are reference material to help:
- New contributors understand pointer events
- Developers debug similar shadow DOM issues
- Teams understand spec-compliant event dispatch
- Future maintainers understand the fix rationale

prs.title: Mishti_Agarwal_assignment
prs.body:
# SAST Rocket Launch Countdown Timer 🚀

A modern, responsive countdown timer application for the SAST (Student Association of Science and Technology) rocket launch event scheduled for December 1, 2025.

## Features

- **Live Countdown Display**: Real-time countdown showing Days, Hours, Minutes, and Seconds
- **Modern UI**: Beautiful glassmorphism design with gradient background and smooth animations
- **Responsive Design**: Works seamlessly on desktop, tablet, and mobile devices
- **Launch Animation**: Displays celebratory message when countdown reaches zero
- **Clean Code**: Well-documented JavaScript with constants, helper functions, and JSDoc comments
- **Performance Optimized**: Efficient DOM updates and minimal memory footprint

## Files

- `ROCKET.html` - Main HTML structure with semantic markup
- `ROCKET.css` - Modern styling with animations, gradients, and responsive layout
- `script.js` - Countdown logic with refactored, maintainable code

## Launch Date

**December 1, 2025 @ 6:00 PM**

## Technologies Used

- HTML5
- CSS3 (with animations and backdrop filters)
- Vanilla JavaScript (ES6+)

## How to Use

1. Open `ROCKET.html` in your web browser
2. Watch the countdown timer update in real-time
3. See the launch message when the countdown reaches zero

## Code Quality

- JSDoc documentation for all functions
- Semantic variable naming
- Modular helper functions
- CSS animations and effects
- Mobile-first responsive design

---

prs.title: Crystal tower Library
prs.body:
In the Crystal Tower Library, crystals are arranged on shelves following a magical pattern. The first shelf has 1 crystal, the second shelf also has 1 crystal, and from the third shelf onward, the number of crystals equals the sum of the previous two shelves.

**Logic:**
This pattern follows the Fibonacci sequence:
`F(1) = 1`, `F(2) = 1`, and `F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2)` for `n > 2`.
To find the number of crystals on the n-th shelf, calculate the n-th Fibonacci number.

**Result:**
Return the value of `F(n)`, which represents the number of crystals on the n-th shelf.

prs.title: Be Positive
prs.body:
**Description:**
You are given an array `a` of size `n`, where each element is either `-1`, `0`, or `1`. You can perform an operation where you increase any element by `1`. The goal is to make the product of all numbers in the array **greater than 0** using the fewest possible operations.

**Logic:**

* Each `0` must be turned into `1`, so that takes one operation per zero.
* If the number of `-1`s is even, the product will be positive after fixing zeros.
* If the number of `-1`s is odd, you need to turn one `-1` into `1`, which takes two operations.

**Result:**
The minimum number of operations required is:
`number of zeros + (2 if number of -1s is odd else 0)`

prs.title: Distinct Element
prs.body:
This problem is about reconstructing an array ( a ) from a given array ( b ). The array ( b ) is defined using the count of distinct elements in subarrays of ( a ). Specifically, each ( b_i ) is the sum of distinct element counts across all subarrays that end at position ( i ). You are guaranteed that at least one valid array ( a ) exists, and you must find any one possible configuration of ( a ) that satisfies the given conditions.

To solve this, we observe that the difference between consecutive elements of ( b ) (that is, ( d_i = b_i - b_{i-1} )) tells us how far back in the array the previous occurrence of the same element should be. If ( d_i = i ), it means this element has not appeared before and starts a new distinct chain. If ( d_i < i ), it means the current element repeats the same value as the element at position ( i - d_i ). Using this logic, we can reconstruct ( a ) step by step.

The algorithm runs efficiently with ( O(n) ) complexity for each test case. We start from the first element and compute ( d_i ) for each position, deciding whether to assign a new number or repeat a previous one. Since there can be at most ( n ) distinct numbers, all values of ( a_i ) lie within the range ( [1, n] ). This guarantees a valid solution that satisfies all problem constraints.

prs.title: Sublime
prs.body:
Farmer John has an integer x and creates a sequence of length n by alternating x and −x, starting with x. For example, if n = 5 and x = 3, the sequence is 3, −3, 3, −3, 3. The goal is to find the sum of all numbers in this sequence.

The input consists of multiple test cases. Each test case provides two integers — x and n. The output for each test case is the total sum of the sequence formed by alternating x and −x for n terms.

The logic is simple: if n is even, the number of positive and negative terms is equal, so the sum is 0. If n is odd, there is one extra positive x, so the sum equals x. This makes the solution quick and efficient using just a parity check on n.

prs.title: FROG GAME
prs.body:
The Lilypad Game is a turn-based strategy problem where two players, Alice and Bob, move alternately across a row of lilypads numbered from 1 to n. Alice starts on lilypad a, and Bob starts on lilypad b. In each move, a player can step one position left or right unless that would take them off the board or onto the lilypad occupied by the other player. The player unable to move loses immediately, and both play optimally.
The input consists of several test cases. Each test case provides three integers — n (number of lilypads), a (Alice’s starting position), and b (Bob’s starting position). The output for each test case is either “Alice” or “Bob”, indicating the winner assuming both play perfectly. The outcome depends on the parity of the number of lilypads separating them — if the distance between them is even, Alice can force a win; if odd, Bob can.
This problem highlights game theory and parity-based reasoning. Since both players move alternately and symmetrically, the winner can be determined solely by whether the number of available spaces between them is even or odd. It’s a simple yet insightful exercise in understanding optimal play, logical deduction, and turn-based problem-solving.

prs.title: Circle Intersection Check With Different Radii
prs.body:
The Circle Intersection Check With Different Radii problem focuses on determining the relationship between two circles placed on a 2D plane. Each circle is represented by its center coordinates and radius. The task is to identify whether the circles intersect, touch at a single point, or are completely separate from each other.

The program takes the coordinates of both circle centers along with their radii as input. Using the distance formula, it calculates how far apart the centers are. Based on this distance, it checks specific geometric conditions and prints the result — whether the circles intersect, touch, or do not meet at all.

This problem combines geometry and logical reasoning to solve a real-world mathematical scenario. It helps in understanding the concept of distances and circle properties while improving skills in condition-based programming. Such problems are widely used in areas like graphics, physics simulations, and collision detection systems.

prs.title: Newbm
Password Check
prs.body:
The Password Check problem involves verifying whether a given password meets specific security requirements. The program takes a password as input and determines if it follows the defined rules, such as having a minimum length, containing both uppercase and lowercase letters, including digits or special characters, and not having spaces or disallowed symbols. The goal is to ensure that the entered password is strong and secure.

⚙️ Input and Output

The input consists of a single string representing the user’s password. The program checks the password against the given conditions and outputs whether it is valid or invalid. In some implementations, it may also specify which criteria the password fails to meet (for example: "Password must contain at least one uppercase letter").

💡 Key Concept

The problem emphasizes understanding string manipulation, character checking, and conditional logic in programming. By validating different properties of the string, the program helps simulate real-world password verification systems. This task is an excellent beginner exercise for practicing loops, conditional statements, and input validation techniques.

prs.title: MEX rose
prs.body:
The task is to determine the minimum number of operations needed to make the MEX (minimum excluded number) of an array equal to a given value k. The MEX of an array is defined as the smallest non-negative integer that is not present in the array. In each operation, you can choose any index and set its value to any integer between 0 and n. This problem requires understanding how the structure of the array affects its MEX value
.
You are given multiple test cases. Each test case provides two integers — the length of the array n and the target k, followed by the array elements. For every test case, the output should be a single integer representing the minimum number of operations required to make MEX(a) = k. The constraints ensure that the total sum of array sizes across all test cases does not exceed 2*10**5, allowing efficient computation.

To make the MEX equal to k, two conditions must be met: all integers from 0 to k−1 must appear in the array, and k itself must not appear. The solution involves counting how many of the numbers below k are missing and whether k is present. The total number of these necessary changes gives the minimum number of operations. This problem builds logical reasoning around the concept of MEX and is often featured in competitive programming contests like Codeforces.

prs.title: A SQUARE
prs.body:
This program determines whether it is possible to form a square using four given sticks. For each test case, the user provides the lengths of the four sticks, and the program checks if all sides are equal. If all four lengths are the same, then it is possible to construct a perfect square; otherwise, it is not.
The input begins with an integer t, representing the number of test cases. For each test case, four integers a, b, c, and d are given — the lengths of the sticks. The program outputs "YES" if the sticks can form a square, and "NO" otherwise. The output is case-insensitive, meaning "yes", "Yes", and "YES" are all valid affirmative responses.
The logic behind the solution is simple — a square requires all four sides to be of equal length. By comparing all the stick lengths, we can quickly determine whether they meet this condition. This problem helps beginners practice conditional statements, loops, and input handling in Python while reinforcing basic logical reasoning.

prs.title: interview question
prs.body:
The **Armstrong Numbers in Range (L to R)** program is a Python-based function that identifies all Armstrong numbers within a given range. An Armstrong number is a number that equals the sum of its own digits each raised to the power of the number of digits. For example, `153 = 1³ + 5³ + 3³`, making it an Armstrong number.

This function takes two inputs, `L` (lower limit) and `R` (upper limit), and checks every number in that range. For each number, it calculates the sum of its digits raised to the appropriate power and compares the result to the original number. If they match, the number is added to the output list of Armstrong numbers.

The project provides a simple, beginner-friendly approach to understanding Python loops, functions, and numeric logic. It can serve as a foundational exercise in algorithmic thinking and can be extended further for advanced number theory or mathematical problem-solving applications.

prs.title: message

issues.title: Landing Page Loads External Font Resources Incorrectly
issues.body: ## Description The landing page is not loading all external font resources correctly. While testing the application locally, multiple font-related stylesheet requests on the landing page did not load successfully, which may affect typography rendering and visual consistency across different environments. ## Steps to Reproduce 1. Start the application locally. 2. Navigate to the landing page (`/home/`). 3. Open browser Developer Tools. 4. Go to the Network tab and filter by CSS resources. 5. Refresh the page. ## Actual Behavior Some font-related stylesheet requests do not load successfully. ## Expected Behavior All font-related stylesheet resources should load successfully without network errors. ## Environment * Browser: Firefox * Local Development Environment * Operating System: macOS ## Additional Context The issue is reproducible in a clean browser session and can be observed through the browser's network monitoring tools.

issues.title: Duplicate theme.js import on landing page causes duplicate theme toggle initialization
issues.body:
The landing page imports `theme.js` twice, causing duplicate initialization of the theme toggle functionality.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open the landing page.
2. Inspect the loaded scripts.
3. Observe that `theme.js` is included twice.
4. Inspect `#themeToggle` event listeners.

## Expected Behavior

The theme script should be loaded once and initialize a single click handler.

## Actual Behavior

The script is loaded twice, resulting in duplicate click listener registration and inconsistent theme toggle behavior.

## Root Cause

`game/templates/game/landing.html` contains two imports of `theme.js`:

* `{% static 'game/js/theme.js' %}`
* `{% static 'game/js/theme.js' %}?v=1`

Removing the duplicate import resolves the issue.

## Fix

Remove the duplicate script import and retain a single versioned import.

issues.title: [Feature]: Improve recommendation input validation for unsupported values
issues.body:
### What problem does this solve?

Currently, recommendation input validation checks for missing fields but does not validate unsupported values.

For example:

* Invalid time availability (`"very high"`, `"2 hours"`)
* Unsupported experience levels
* Unsupported interest values

This can lead to inconsistent API behavior and potential runtime errors during recommendation scoring.


### Proposed solution

Add stricter validation for allowed values in recommendation inputs.

Validate:

* `level` → Beginner / Intermediate / Advanced
* `time` → Low / Medium / High
* `interest` → supported interests only
* `skills` → non-empty and properly formatted

Also:

* Add tests covering invalid input cases
* Return meaningful API error messages with proper status codes


### Alternatives considered

No additional validation and rely on current missing-field checks, but this may allow unsupported values to reach recommendation logic and create inconsistent behavior.

### Which part of the app does this affect?

Recommendation engine (backend)

### Estimated complexity

Medium (one function and one template change)

### Before submitting

- [x] I searched existing issues and this has not been requested before.
- [x] This change would benefit contributors beyond my own use case.

issues.title: ENH: Add model comparison forecasting example notebook
issues.body:
### Describe the problem
Current forecasting examples demonstrate single-model usage, but do not clearly show how to compare multiple forecasting models.

### Proposed solution
Add a new notebook example that:
- trains multiple models (Theta, Naive, ExponentialSmoothing)
- compares their performance using MAE
- selects the best model

### Why this is useful
This improves usability for beginners and demonstrates practical model selection workflows in sktime.

issues.title: [Bug] TypeError when creating new project from Planet interface
issues.body: ### Description When creating a new project from the Planet interface, a runtime error occurs in the browser console. The error appears when clicking the "+" (New Project) button after opening the Planet interface. The issue seems to originate from js/planetInterface.js, where this.activity._loadStart() is called, but _loadStart() does not appear to exist on the activity object. ### Expected Behavior Clicking the "+" (New Project) button should create a new project without any runtime errors in the console. ### Screenshots ### How to Reproduce 1.Run Music Blocks locally 2.Open the application in the browser (http://127.0.0.1:3000) 3.Click the Planet (globe) icon 4.In the Planet interface, click the "+" (New Project) button 5.Open the browser developer console ### Console log Errors: ### Environment: - Operating System: macOS - Browser: Chrome - Version of Software/Project: Latest master branch (local development) ### Checklist - [x] I have read and followed the project's code of conduct. - [x] I have searched for similar issues before creating this one. - [x] I have provided all the necessary information to understand and reproduce the issue. - [x] I am willing to contribute to the resolution of this issue. --- Thank you for contributing to our project! We appreciate your help in improving it. 📚 See [contributing instructions](https://github.com/sugarlabs/musicblocks/blob/master/README.md). 🙋🏾🙋🏼 Questions: [Community Matrix Server](https://matrix.to/#/#sugar:matrix.org).

issues.title: Improve empty state UI when no upcoming events are available
issues.body: ### Problem When there are no upcoming events on the homepage, the interface only shows the message: "No upcoming events available yet." This creates a poor user experience because new users are not guided on what to do next. ### Expected Behavior When no events are available, the page should display a helpful call-to-action such as: "Create your first event". ### Proposed Solution Add a call-to-action button below the message that redirects users to the event creation page. Example: Create your first event → `/control/events/create` ### Benefits * Improves onboarding experience * Helps users understand the next step * Enhances overall UX ### Screenshots Current behavior (from local development setup):

issues.title: [Chore] Improve error handling for missing input connections in block execution
issues.body:
### Current Behavior

Some block implementations check for missing input connections but return
generic errors or unexpected values.

When an input connection is null or missing, the block execution may fail
without providing a clear explanation of what went wrong.

### Desired Situation

Blocks should consistently check for missing input connections and provide
clear error messages when inputs are missing.

Improved error handling would ensure blocks fail gracefully and help users
debug their programs more easily.

### Checklist

- [x] I have read and followed the project's code of conduct.
- [x] I have searched for similar issues before creating this one.
- [x] I have provided all the necessary information to understand the issue.
- [x] I am willing to contribute to the resolution of this issue.

---

Thank you for contributing to our project! We appreciate your help in improving it.

issues.title: [Test] Increase unit test coverage for BooleanBlocks.js
issues.body:
### What needs testing

BooleanBlocks.js requires improved unit test coverage.

Several boolean block flow branches and edge-case conditions are not currently covered by tests.

### Current coverage

Current coverage for BooleanBlocks.js is approximately 35% (based on latest coverage report).

### Proposed approach

I plan to:

- Identify uncovered branches using coverage report
- Add targeted unit tests for:
  - Edge-case boolean inputs
  - Guard conditions
  - Early returns
  - Error-handling paths
- Follow existing block test patterns in js/blocks/__tests__
- Ensure all tests pass and do not affect existing functionality

### Checklist

-   [x] I have read and followed the project's code of conduct.
-   [x] I have checked that no existing tests cover this area.
-   [x] I have checked that there is no open PR for the tests I am submitting.
-   [x] I have reviewed [docs/TESTING.md](docs/TESTING.md) for testing patterns.

---

📚 See [Testing Guide](docs/TESTING.md) for how to write block tests.

🙋🏾🙋🏼 Questions: [Community Matrix Server](https://matrix.to/#/#sugar:matrix.org).

issues.title: [Test] Add unit tests for delayExecution utility function
issues.body:
### What needs testing

The `delayExecution` utility function defined in `js/utils/utils.js`.

It is used across core modules (e.g., `block.js`, `turtle.js`) to implement async delay and retry behavior.

---

### Current coverage

There are currently no dedicated unit tests covering `delayExecution`.

A search inside `js/__tests__/` shows no existing test cases validating its behavior.

---

### Proposed approach

Add a new Jest test file:

`js/__tests__/delayExecution.test.js`

Tests will:

- Use `jest.useFakeTimers()` for deterministic timing
- Verify the Promise resolves correctly
- Confirm that the delay duration is respected
- Ensure multiple calls behave independently
- Validate no unexpected side effects occur

---

### Checklist

- [x] I have read and followed the project's code of conduct.
- [x] I have checked that no existing tests cover this area.
- [x] I have checked that there is no open PR for the tests I am submitting.
- [x] I have reviewed docs/TESTING.md for testing patterns.
